docs.title: Recommend Python Best Practices
docs.text: This document outlines a series of recommended best practices for Python development. These guidelines aim to improve code quality, maintainability, and readability.

docs.title: Imports
docs.text: Use import statements for packages and modules only, not for individual types, classes, or functions.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Reusability mechanism for sharing code from one module to another.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
• Use import x for importing packages and modules.
  • Use from x import y where x is the package prefix and y is the module name with no prefix.
  • Use from x import y as z in any of the following circumstances:
  • Two modules named y are to be imported.
  • y conflicts with a top-level name defined in the current module.
  • y conflicts with a common parameter name that is part of the public API (e.g., features).
  • y is an inconveniently long name, or too generic in the context of your code
  • Use import y as z only when z is a standard abbreviation (e.g., import numpy as np).

For example the module sound.effects.echo may be imported as follows:

from sound.effects import echo
...
echo.EchoFilter(input, output, delay=0.7, atten=4)

Do not use relative names in imports. Even if the module is in the same package, use the full package name. This helps prevent unintentionally importing a package twice.

docs.title: Exemptions
docs.text:
Exemptions from this rule:

  • Symbols from the following modules are used to support static analysis and type checking:
  • typing module
  • collections.abc module
  • typing_extensions module
  • Redirects from the six.moves module.

docs.title: Packages
docs.text: Import each module using the full pathname location of the module.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
All new code should import each module by its full package name.

Imports should be as follows:

Yes:
  # Reference absl.flags in code with the complete name (verbose).
  import absl.flags
  from doctor.who import jodie

  _FOO = absl.flags.DEFINE_string(...)

Yes:
  # Reference flags in code with just the module name (common).
  from absl import flags
  from doctor.who import jodie

  _FOO = flags.DEFINE_string(...)

(assume this file lives in doctor/who/ where jodie.py also exists)

No:
  # Unclear what module the author wanted and what will be imported.  The actual
  # import behavior depends on external factors controlling sys.path.
  # Which possible jodie module did the author intend to import?
  import jodie

The directory the main binary is located in should not be assumed to be in sys.path despite that happening in some environments. This being the case, code should assume that import jodie refers to a third-party or top-level package named jodie, not a local jodie.py.

docs.title: Default Iterators and Operators
docs.text: Use default iterators and operators for types that support them, like lists, dictionaries, and files.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: Container types, like dictionaries and lists, define default iterators and membership test operators (“in” and “not in”).

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
Use default iterators and operators for types that support them, like lists, dictionaries, and files. The built-in types define iterator methods, too. Prefer these methods to methods that return lists, except that you should not mutate a container while iterating over it.

Yes:  for key in adict: ...
      if obj in alist: ...
      for line in afile: ...
      for k, v in adict.items(): ...

No:   for key in adict.keys(): ...
      for line in afile.readlines(): ...

docs.title: Lambda Functions
docs.text: Okay for one-liners. Prefer generator expressions over map() or filter() with a lambda.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
Lambdas are allowed. If the code inside the lambda function spans multiple lines or is longer than 60-80 chars, it might be better to define it as a regular nested function.

For common operations like multiplication, use the functions from the operator module instead of lambda functions. For example, prefer operator.mul to lambda x, y: x * y.

docs.title: Default Argument Values
docs.text: Okay in most cases.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text: You can specify values for variables at the end of a function’s parameter list, e.g., def foo(a, b=0):. If foo is called with only one argument, b is set to 0. If it is called with two arguments, b has the value of the second argument.

docs.title: Decision
docs.text:
Okay to use with the following caveat:

Do not use mutable objects as default values in the function or method definition.

Yes: def foo(a, b=None):
         if b is None:
             b = []
Yes: def foo(a, b: Sequence | None = None):
         if b is None:
             b = []
Yes: def foo(a, b: Sequence = ()):  # Empty tuple OK since tuples are immutable.
         ...

from absl import flags
_FOO = flags.DEFINE_string(...)

No:  def foo(a, b=[]):
         ...
No:  def foo(a, b=time.time()):  # Is `b` supposed to represent when this module was loaded?
         ...
No:  def foo(a, b=_FOO.value):  # sys.argv has not yet been parsed...
         ...
No:  def foo(a, b: Mapping = {}):  # Could still get passed to unchecked code.
         ...

docs.title: True/False Evaluations
docs.text: Use the “implicit” false if possible, e.g., if foo: rather than if foo != []:

docs.title: Lexical Scoping
docs.text:
Okay to use.

An example of the use of this feature is:

def get_adder(summand1: float) -> Callable[[float], float]:
    """Returns a function that adds numbers to a given number."""
    def adder(summand2: float) -> float:
        return summand1 + summand2

    return adder

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Okay to use.

docs.title: Threading
docs.text:
Do not rely on the atomicity of built-in types.

While Python’s built-in data types such as dictionaries appear to have atomic operations, there are corner cases where they aren’t atomic (e.g. if __hash__ or __eq__ are implemented as Python methods) and their atomicity should not be relied upon. Neither should you rely on atomic variable assignment (since this in turn depends on dictionaries).

Use the queue module’s Queue data type as the preferred way to communicate data between threads. Otherwise, use the threading module and its locking primitives. Prefer condition variables and threading.Condition instead of using lower-level locks.

docs.title: Seeing what's configured
docs.text:
All available settings live in the config reference. Per-tool options are documented on each tool's page.

To print the current config as a PR comment:

/config

To see the actual resolved config for a specific tool (after all overrides are applied):

/improve --config.output_relevant_configurations=true

docs.title: Ignoring files
docs.text: Skip generated code, vendored dependencies, or anything else you don't want reviewed.

docs.title: Glob patterns
docs.text:
/review --ignore.glob="['*.py']"

Or in config:

[ignore]
glob = ['*.py']

docs.title: Regex patterns
docs.text:
[ignore]
regex = ['.*\\.py$']

docs.title: Extra instructions
docs.text:
Every tool accepts free-text guidance via extra_instructions:

/update_changelog --pr_update_changelog.extra_instructions="Also bump the version number"

docs.title: Response language
docs.text:
MergeMate defaults to U.S. English. To switch:

[config]
response_language = "ja-JP"

Uses ISO 639 / ISO 3166 locale codes. Full locale list →

Only AI-generated text is translated. Static labels and table headers stay in English. Make sure your model supports the target language well.

docs.title: GitLab submodule diffs
docs.text:
By default, GitLab shows submodule changes as a single Subproject commit line. To expand those into full diffs:

[gitlab]
expand_submodule_diffs = true

This adds extra API calls, so it's off by default.

docs.title: Log level
docs.text:
Control verbosity for debugging:

[config]
log_level = "DEBUG"  # DEBUG | INFO | WARNING | ERROR | CRITICAL

Default is DEBUG.

docs.title: Observability platforms
docs.text:
MergeMate uses LiteLLM under the hood, so any LiteLLM-compatible observability tool works. Example with LangSmith:

[litellm]
enable_callbacks = true
success_callback = ["langsmith"]
failure_callback = ["langsmith"]

LANGSMITH_API_KEY=<key>
LANGSMITH_PROJECT=<project>
LANGSMITH_BASE_URL=<url>

docs.title: Repository metadata
docs.text:
Let MergeMate discover project-wide context automatically:

[config]
add_repo_metadata = true

It scans the PR's head branch for AGENTS.MD, CLAUDE.MD, and other metadata files.

Custom file list:

[config]
add_repo_metadata_file_list = ["CONTRIBUTING.md", "ARCHITECTURE.md"]

docs.title: By title
docs.text:
[config]
ignore_pr_title = ["\\[Bump\\]"]

Regex patterns. Default: ["^\\[Auto\\]", "^Auto"].

docs.title: By branch
docs.text:
[config]
ignore_pr_source_branches = ['develop', 'main', 'master', 'stage']
ignore_pr_target_branches = ["qa"]

docs.title: By repository
docs.text:
[config]
ignore_repositories = ["my-org/my-repo1", "my-org/my-repo2"]

docs.title: By folder (monorepo allowlist)
docs.text:
[config]
allow_only_specific_folders = ['services/api', 'packages/core']

Only PRs touching files in these folders get automatic feedback.

docs.title: By label
docs.text:
[config]
ignore_pr_labels = ["do-not-merge"]

docs.title: By author
docs.text:
MergeMate auto-detects bots using GitHub's bot flag and common naming patterns. To add manual overrides:

[config]
ignore_pr_authors = ["my-bot-user", "dependabot"]

Regex list. Note: bots that create PRs with failing tests will still get a response.

docs.title: By generated file type
docs.text:
[config]
ignore_language_framework = ['protobuf']

Patterns come from generated_code_ignore.toml.

docs.title: By ticket label
docs.text:
When MergeMate pulls ticket context (JIRA, GitHub Issues, etc.), skip tickets with certain labels:

[config]
ignore_ticket_labels = ["ignore-compliance", "skip-review", "wont-fix"]

docs.title: CLI
docs.text:
Fire off tools from your terminal. Your local config drives everything.

# Review a PR
python -m mergemate.cli --pr_url=https://github.com/owner/repo/pull/50 review

# Generate a description
python -m mergemate.cli --pr_url=https://github.com/owner/repo/pull/50 describe

# Suggest improvements
python -m mergemate.cli --pr_url=https://github.com/owner/repo/pull/50 improve

# Ask anything
python -m mergemate.cli --pr_url=https://github.com/owner/repo/pull/50 ask "Summarize the security impact of this PR"

# Update the changelog
python -m mergemate.cli --pr_url=https://github.com/owner/repo/pull/50 update_changelog

Pro tips:

Override any config value inline:

python -m mergemate.cli --pr_url=<url> review --pr_reviewer.extra_instructions="Focus on error handling"

Run locally without publishing (great for testing):

[config]
publish_output = false
verbosity_level = 2

Git providers: set the git_provider field to github (default), gitlab, bitbucket, azure, codecommit, local, or gitea.

docs.title: Health check
docs.text:
Confirm everything is wired up:

python -m tests.health_test.main

You'll need an LLM provider configured and a valid token set before running this.

docs.title: Online usage
docs.text:
Trigger MergeMate by commenting on a PR:

Command What it does /review Full code review /describe PR summary + labels /improve Code suggestions /ask "..." Ask a question about the PR /update_changelog Update the changelog

Tweak config on the fly:

/review --pr_reviewer.extra_instructions="..." --pr_reviewer.require_score_review=false

Any setting from the config file can be overridden this way. Run /config to see what's available.

docs.title: Kill switch
docs.text:
To silence all automatic feedback across every platform:

[config]
disable_auto_feedback = true

No tools will fire on PR open or push events.

docs.title: On new PRs
docs.text:
Define which tools run automatically when a PR opens:

[github_app]
pr_commands = [
    "/describe",
    "/review",
    "/improve",
]

This fires on open, reopen, and ready-for-review transitions.

Draft PRs are skipped by default. To include them:

[github_app]
feedback_on_draft_pr = true

Customize tool params for auto-runs:

[pr_description]
generate_ai_title = true

Or target specific automated runs:

[github_app]
pr_commands = [
    "/describe",
    "/review --pr_reviewer.extra_instructions='Focus on SQL injection patterns'",
    "/improve",
]

Configuration follows the same precedence rules (wiki → local → global).

docs.title: On new commits
docs.text:
Respond to pushes on open PRs:

[github_app]
handle_push_trigger = true
push_commands = [
    "/describe",
    "/review",
]

docs.title: GitHub Action
docs.text:
GitHub Actions use environment variables, not the app config:

env:
  OPENAI_KEY: ${{ secrets.OPENAI_KEY }}
  GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
  github_action_config.auto_review: "true"
  github_action_config.auto_describe: "true"
  github_action_config.auto_improve: "true"
  github_action_config.pr_actions: '["opened", "reopened", "ready_for_review", "review_requested"]'

The three auto_* flags control what runs on PR open. Default: all three are on.

pr_actions sets which pull request events trigger the automation. Default: ["opened", "reopened", "ready_for_review", "review_requested"].

github_action_config.enable_output toggles the action's JSON output parameter (default true).

You can also drop a .mergemate.toml in your repo root for additional config, or set more env vars:

pr_description.publish_labels: "false"

docs.title: Enable PR comment triggers
docs.text:
Add issue_comment events to your workflow:

on:
  issue_comment:
    types: [created, edited]

docs.title: Quick model config for GitHub Actions
docs.text:
Model Key env vars OpenAI config.model: "gpt-5.4", OPENAI_KEY Gemini config.model: "gemini/gemini-1.5-flash", GOOGLE_AI_STUDIO.GEMINI_API_KEY Claude config.model: "anthropic/claude-3-opus-20240229", ANTHROPIC.KEY Azure OpenAI OPENAI.API_TYPE: "azure", OPENAI.API_BASE, OPENAI.DEPLOYMENT_ID Local (Ollama) config.model: "ollama/model-name", OLLAMA.API_BASE

Environment variable format: use dots for nesting (config.model), strings for booleans ("true"), and JSON strings for arrays ('["a", "b"]').

Full model setup guide: Changing a model →

docs.title: GitLab Webhook
docs.text:
Same pattern as GitHub App:

[gitlab]
pr_commands = [
    "/describe",
    "/review",
    "/improve",
]

Push-trigger support:

[gitlab]
handle_push_trigger = true
push_commands = [
    "/describe",
    "/review",
]

Make sure your webhook has "Push events" scope enabled for handle_push_trigger.

docs.title: Bitbucket App
docs.text:
Bitbucket loads config from a .mergemate.toml file at the root of your repo's default branch. Upload it before creating PRs.

Example local overrides:

[pr_reviewer]
extra_instructions = "Answer in Japanese"

Rate limit note: Bitbucket caps app requests at ~1000/hour with no usage API. If you get spotty responses, try:

bitbucket_app.avoid_full_files = true

This skips full-file fetches (slight accuracy trade-off, fewer API calls).

docs.title: Auto-run on PR open
docs.text:
[bitbucket_app]
pr_commands = [
    "/review",
    "/improve --pr_code_suggestions.commitable_code_suggestions=true --pr_code_suggestions.suggestions_score_threshold=7",
]

We recommend suggestions_score_threshold=7 for Bitbucket since it only supports inline suggestions — fewer, higher-quality hits.

Push-trigger:

[bitbucket_app]
handle_push_trigger = true
push_commands = [
    "/describe",
    "/review",
]

docs.title: Azure DevOps
docs.text:
Set the provider and auth in config:

[config]
git_provider = "azure"

Two auth options:

  • PAT token — quicker setup, inherits your user identity, expires by design
  • DefaultAzureCredential — managed identity or service principal, more secure, separate identity

Secrets go in .secrets.toml:

[azure_devops]
org = "https://dev.azure.com/YOUR_ORGANIZATION/"
# pat = "YOUR_PAT_TOKEN"  # only if using PAT auth

For DefaultAzureCredential, set AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET (or use managed identity / az login locally).

docs.title: Webhook auto-run
docs.text:
[azure_devops_server]
pr_commands = [
    "/describe",
    "/review",
    "/improve",
]

docs.title: Gitea Webhook
docs.text:
[gitea]
pr_commands = [
    "/describe",
    "/review",
    "/improve",
]

docs.title: Switching AI models
docs.text:
MergeMate ships with GPT-5 as the default. You can swap it for any model LiteLLM supports.

Set the model in your config:

[config]
model = "your-model-here"
fallback_models = ["fallback-if-primary-fails"]

Provider-specific env vars

Check the LiteLLM docs for the exact environment variables each model needs. Missing keys usually result in LiteLLM failing to identify the model type.

docs.title: OpenAI
docs.text:
# .secrets.toml
[openai]
api_base = "https://api.openai.com/v1"
api_key = "sk-..."

Or use env vars (note the double underscore):

OPENAI__API_BASE=https://api.openai.com/v1
OPENAI__KEY=sk-...

docs.title: OpenAI Flex Processing
docs.text:
Reduce costs on background tasks:

[litellm]
extra_body = '{"processing_mode": "flex"}'

docs.title: Azure
docs.text:
# .secrets.toml
[openai]
key = ""
api_type = "azure"
api_version = "2023-05-15"
api_base = "https://<resource>.openai.azure.com"
deployment_id = "<deployment-name>"

# configuration.toml
[config]
model = "gpt-4o"
fallback_models = ["gpt-4o"]

docs.title: Azure AD (Entra ID)
docs.text:
# .secrets.toml
[azure_ad]
client_id = ""
client_secret = ""
tenant_id = ""
api_base = "https://openai.xyz.com/"

docs.title: Custom headers
docs.text:
Route through an API gateway:

[litellm]
extra_headers = '{"projectId": "<id>"}'

docs.title: Ollama (local)
docs.text:
# configuration.toml
[config]
model = "ollama/qwen2.5-coder:32b"
fallback_models = ["ollama/qwen2.5-coder:32b"]
custom_model_max_tokens = 128000
duplicate_examples = true

# .secrets.toml
[ollama]
api_base = "http://localhost:11434"

Ollama defaults to a 2048-token context window — way too small for PR diffs. Bump it up:

OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH=8192 ollama serve

Make sure custom_model_max_tokens matches your OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH.

Local vs. cloud models

MergeMate works with almost any model, but PR analysis is demanding. GPT-5, Claude Sonnet, and Gemini handle it well. Most open-source models (as of early 2025) struggle with the structured output and large inputs this task requires.

Local models are fine for experimenting (especially with /ask), but stick to commercial models for production workflows.

docs.title: Anthropic
docs.text:
[config]
model = "anthropic/claude-3-opus-20240229"
fallback_models = ["anthropic/claude-3-opus-20240229"]

# .secrets.toml
[anthropic]
KEY = "..."

docs.title: Google AI Studio
docs.text:
[config]
model = "gemini/gemini-1.5-flash"
fallback_models = ["gemini/gemini-1.5-flash"]

# .secrets.toml
[google_ai_studio]
gemini_api_key = "..."

Or set GOOGLE_AI_STUDIO.GEMINI_API_KEY as an env var.

docs.title: Vertex AI
docs.text:
[config]
model = "vertex_ai/codechat-bison"
fallback_models = ["vertex_ai/codechat-bison"]

# .secrets.toml
[vertexai]
vertex_project = "my-google-cloud-project"
vertex_location = ""

Uses application default credentials. Set GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS if you need explicit auth.

docs.title: Amazon Bedrock
docs.text:
[config]
model = "bedrock/anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620-v1:0"
fallback_models = ["bedrock/anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620-v1:0"]

# .secrets.toml
[aws]
AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID = "..."
AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY = "..."
AWS_REGION_NAME = "..."

docs.title: IAM roles (recommended on AWS)
docs.text:
Running on EC2, ECS, EKS, or Lambda? Skip the static keys and use ambient credentials:

# GitHub Actions
- uses: MergeMate-ai/mergemate@main
  env:
    GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
    AWS_USE_IMDS: "true"
  with:
    command: review

Your IAM role needs:

{
  "Effect": "Allow",
  "Action": "bedrock:InvokeModel",
  "Resource": "arn:aws:bedrock:us-east-1::foundation-model/anthropic.claude-3-5-sonnet-20240620-v1:0"
}

Static keys act as a fallback if ambient credentials fail.

docs.title: Custom inference profiles
docs.text:
[litellm]
model_id = "your-custom-inference-profile-id"

docs.title: Hugging Face
docs.text:
[config]
model = "huggingface/meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-chat-hf"
fallback_models = ["huggingface/meta-llama/Llama-2-7b-chat-hf"]

docs.title: Custom models
docs.text:
If your model isn't in the supported list, you can still use it:

  1. Set the name: 

    [config]
model = "custom_model_name"
fallback_models = ["custom_model_name"]

  2. Set the max tokens: 

    custom_model_max_tokens = ...

  3. Configure the env vars per LiteLLM's docs.

  4. For reasoning models that don't support chat-style inputs: 

    custom_reasoning_model = true

docs.title: OpenAI reasoning effort
docs.text:
[config]
reasoning_effort = "medium"  # none, minimal, low, medium, high, xhigh

docs.title: Anthropic extended thinking
docs.text:
[config]
enable_claude_extended_thinking = false
extended_thinking_budget_tokens = 2048
extended_thinking_max_output_tokens = 4096

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
MergeMate reads its settings from a TOML file. You can place it in four locations, ranked by priority:

Layer Scope Overrides 1. Wiki Single repo (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket) Everything below 2. Local (.mergemate.toml in repo root) Single repo Global + external 3. Global (mergemate-settings repo) Entire org / project / group External URL 4. External URL (CLI flag) Any Nothing (applied first)

Keep it minimal

Only set the values you need to change. Copying the full defaults file creates maintenance headaches later.

Debug your config

Set config.output_relevant_configurations = true to see exactly which settings each tool is using — printed in a collapsible section in the output.

docs.title: Wiki config
docs.text:
Create a wiki page called .mergemate.toml in your repo. No commits needed — just edit and save.

Wrap your config in triple backticks for clean rendering:

```toml
[pr_description]
generate_ai_title = true
```

MergeMate strips the markdown wrapper automatically.

docs.title: Local config
docs.text:
Drop a .mergemate.toml file in the root of your repo's default branch. Upload it before running tools for the config to take effect.

[pr_reviewer]
extra_instructions = """\
- Check for SQL injection
- Verify error handling
"""

docs.title: Loading from a non-default branch
docs.text:
Platform: GitHub

By default, MergeMate reads .mergemate.toml from the default branch. Point it elsewhere with:

python -m mergemate.cli \
  --pr_url=<URL> \
  --config-branch=<branch-name> \
  review

Or set MERGEMATE_CONFIG_BRANCH. The CLI flag wins. If the file isn't found on the requested branch, MergeMate logs a warning and falls back to default.

Security: config branch is a trust boundary

By default, only people who can merge to the default branch control MergeMate's behavior. --config-branch moves that trust boundary.

Never set it from untrusted input like $GITHUB_HEAD_REF. An attacker could supply their own .mergemate.toml and redirect model calls, inject instructions, or enable auto-approval. Always pin it to a maintainer-controlled branch.

GitHub only

Branch selection is currently GitHub-only. Other platforms ignore the flag and always read from the default branch.

docs.title: Global config
docs.text:
Create a repo called mergemate-settings in your org. Every repo in that org inherits its .mergemate.toml.

Local .mergemate.toml files override it per-repo.

Example: https://github.com/imtiyaazsalie/mergemate-settings serves as the global config for all repos under the mergemate-ai org.

docs.title: Project / Group config
docs.text:
Platforms: GitLab, Bitbucket Data Center

Create a mergemate-settings repo inside a GitLab group/subgroup or Bitbucket project. It applies to all repos directly under that group/project.

For GitLab, if a repo is nested under multiple subgroups, MergeMate only looks one level up.

docs.title: Organization-level config
docs.text:
Platform: Bitbucket Data Center

Create a project named MERGEMATE_SETTINGS (key: MERGEMATE_SETTINGS), add a mergemate-settings repo, and put your .mergemate.toml inside. Every repo across every project inherits it.

Project-level settings override organization-level. Repo-local .mergemate.toml beats both.

docs.title: External config URL
docs.text:
Platforms: all

Pass an additional config file from any URL or local path. Applied before all other layers — it acts as a base default.

python -m mergemate.cli \
  --pr_url=<URL> \
  --extra_config_url=https://config.example.com/shared.toml \
  review

Accepted schemes: https://, http://, file://, or a bare path.

docs.title: Private endpoints
docs.text:
Supply an auth header:

export MERGEMATE_EXTRA_CONFIG_AUTH_HEADER="PRIVATE-TOKEN: <your-token>"
export MERGEMATE_EXTRA_CONFIG_AUTH_HEADER="Authorization: Bearer <your-token>"

docs.title: Full precedence chain
docs.text:
built-in defaults
  ← --extra_config_url
    ← global mergemate-settings
      ← local .mergemate.toml (repo default branch)
        ← wiki .mergemate.toml
          ← environment variables (MERGEMATE__SECTION__KEY)

docs.title: Security limits
docs.text:
• Max response size: 1 MB
  • Request timeout: 10 seconds
  • Only http, https, file schemes accepted
  • No executable directives (includes, custom loaders)

If the fetch fails, MergeMate logs it and moves on.

docs.title: Getting started
docs.text:
Once you've installed MergeMate, there are three ways to run it:

  1. CLI — run commands directly from your terminal
  2. Online — trigger commands with PR comments{:target="_blank"}
  3. Auto-pilot — let MergeMate fire on every new PR

For the CLI, you can use our Docker image or run from source.

For online and automatic modes, you'll need to hook up a platform integration: GitHub App, GitHub Action, GitLab webhook, or Bitbucket App. Once connected, MergeMate can respond to comments and auto-run on PR events.

docs.title: Managing email notifications
docs.text: GitHub doesn't let you mute notifications from a single user. If MergeMate's PR comments are filling your inbox, here are your options.

docs.title: Turn off PR comment notifications
docs.text: The simplest fix — disable comment notifications for the repo:

docs.title: Filter in your mail client
docs.text: Create a rule that archives or labels emails from the MergeMate bot. Gmail instructions →

docs.title: Trim the comment size
docs.text:
MergeMate includes a collapsible help section at the bottom of each comment. Turn it off per-tool:

[pr_reviewer]
enable_help_text = false

Smaller comments → shorter notification emails. Repeat for any tool where you want to strip the help block.
